Metadata.Sector: Corporate / Employment / Fiduciary Duties / Technology Manufacturing

Party_Details.Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is part of a worldwide group of companies known as the ABB Group which manufacture, market and sell products for industrial and commercial uses, including low, medium and high voltage circuit breakers.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is a Singapore company owned by ABB Asea Brown Boveri Ltd, a Swiss company, and was set up in Singapore to be the holding company of the Singapore businesses of the ABB Group.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is the sole shareholder of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
ABB Holdings Pte Ltd does not itself undertake commercial activity and functions as a holding company within the ABB Singapore Group.

On 19 July 1996 ABB Holdings Pte Ltd entered into an employment agreement with Sher Hock Guan Charles covering the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000, under which he was to serve as President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.

The 19 July 1996 employment agreement between ABB Holdings Pte Ltd and Sher Hock Guan Charles provided that he was subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation and that he would use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.

On 20 September 1999 ABB Holdings Pte Ltd issued a second employment contract, effective 1 February 2000, stating that the employing entity was ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and that Sher Hock Guan Charles held the position of President.

The 20 September 1999 employment contract provided that Sher Hock Guan Charles would maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters, not divulge information to outside members, and that private participation in other business activities, especially in ABB's line of business, was strictly not allowed.

The ABB Singapore Group maintained an Employee Handbook which set out policies, terms and conditions applicable to all employees, including provisions on private participation in other business activities, transfer and deployment, protection of company assets and confidential information, and avoidance of conflicts of interest.

The Employee Handbook stated that all appointments in ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd were subject to the terms and conditions appearing in the Handbook.
Evidence was given that the Employee Handbook was a 'live document' with regular updating of terms and conditions and that it was made available to all employees of the ABB Singapore Group.
Sher Hock Guan Charles acknowledged in evidence that he was aware of and had access to the Employee Handbook at all material times and that it governed his practice in the ABB Singapore companies.
The ABB Singapore Group included ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, ABB Industry Pte Ltd and other Singapore entities.

In December 2007 ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, together with ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd, commenced Suit 798 of 2007 against Sher Hock Guan Charles in the High Court of Singapore.

By consent of the parties, the trial in Suit 798 of 2007 was focused solely on issues relating to Sher Hock Guan Charles' liability, with quantum to be determined separately if liability was established.

In the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the employment contracts and the Employee Handbook imposed express duties on Sher Hock Guan Charles, including duties to avoid conflicts of interest, refrain from participating in competing business activities, and protect confidential and proprietary information.

In the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded implied duties of good faith and fidelity, duties not to misuse confidential information or trade secrets, and duties to disclose potential threats or competitive risks to their business.

In the alternative, the plaintiffs pleaded that Sher Hock Guan Charles owed fiduciary duties to act in good faith and in the best interests of the plaintiffs, to avoid conflicts between duty and personal interest, and to disclose information of concern to the plaintiffs.

Sher Hock Guan Charles filed a defence denying the existence of most of the pleaded express and implied duties, save for specific clauses in the 1996 and 1999 employment agreements, and denying the alleged breaches.
The High Court reserved judgment and delivered its written decision in ABB Holdings Pte Ltd and Others v Sher Hock Guan Charles [2009] SGHC 157 on 6 July 2009.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether and to what extent the employment contracts and Employee Handbook imposed contractual and fiduciary duties on Sher Hock Guan Charles in favour of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd and the other plaintiffs, and whether his pre- and post-resignation conduct in relation to other businesses and Huadian created liability to ABB Holdings Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Contractual duties arise from express and implied terms of the employment agreements and any incorporated documents such as an employee handbook, construed according to ordinary contractual principles and the parties' intention; fiduciary duties arise where a person undertakes to act for or on behalf of another in circumstances giving rise to a relationship of trust and confidence, particularly for directors and very senior managers, and include duties of loyalty, good faith, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and duties to disclose information of concern to the principal.
Party_Details.Application: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd was a holding company that did not itself conduct commercial operations, and Sher Hock Guan Charles was not employed by it as an operating manager or director; while the first employment contract was issued by ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, it expressly identified ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd as the employing entity, and subsequent contracts and the Employee Handbook focused on duties owed to the operating companies rather than to the holding company; the evidence did not show that he exercised managerial powers or assumed responsibilities on behalf of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd that would give rise to a separate fiduciary relationship with that entity.
Party_Details.Conclusion: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd did not obtain relief against Sher Hock Guan Charles, and its claim was dismissed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined the structure of the ABB Singapore Group and the wording of the employment contracts, noting that ABB Holdings Pte Ltd functioned as a non-operating holding company and that the contracts identified ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and later ABB Industry Pte Ltd as the employing entities; it considered the pleaded duties and the evidence of how the Employee Handbook applied, and concluded that while the Handbook formed part of the employment terms, the fiduciary obligations arising from the defendant's senior management roles were owed to the operating companies rather than to the holding company; the judge also reasoned that there was no evidence of specific powers, responsibilities or reliance that would justify imposing fiduciary duties in favour of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, and therefore the holding company's claim failed even though certain breaches were established vis-à-vis the second and third plaintiffs.

Metadata.Sector: Corporate / Employment / Fiduciary Duties / Technology Manufacturing

Party_Details.Name: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd is part of the ABB Singapore Group and manufactures miniature circuit breakers, which are low voltage products.
ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd is a wholly owned subsidiary of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd.
From January 1997 to January 2000, Sher Hock Guan Charles held the position of President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
From July 1998 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.

During the period when Sher Hock Guan Charles was a director, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd owned and ran two manufacturing plants in Indonesia which assembled miniature circuit breakers.
The manufacturing facility in Batam owned by ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd closed down in 2002.

Under the 19 July 1996 employment contract, Sher Hock Guan Charles was to serve as President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation.

The 20 September 1999 employment contract identified ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd as the employing entity and set out terms including secrecy obligations and a prohibition on private participation in other business activities, especially in ABB's line of business.

The Employee Handbook applicable to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd contained clauses stating that private participation in other business activities especially related to the nature of company business was strictly not allowed, that employees must protect company assets and confidential information, and that employees must avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or cause conflicts with their duties.

By virtue of his position as director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd from July 1998 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles occupied a fiduciary position in relation to that company.

On 15 January 2001, pursuant to a new organisation structure effective 1 January 2001, Sher Hock Guan Charles' employment was transferred from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd, although he remained a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd until February 2003.

In the statement of claim, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and the other plaintiffs pleaded that Sher Hock Guan Charles owed express contractual duties to use his best endeavours to promote their interests, avoid conflicts of interest, refrain from participating in competing business activities, and protect confidential and proprietary information.

The plaintiffs pleaded that Sher Hock Guan Charles owed implied duties of good faith and fidelity, duties not to misuse confidential information or trade secrets, and duties to disclose potential threats or competitive risks posed to their business.

In the alternative, the plaintiffs pleaded that Sher Hock Guan Charles owed fiduciary duties to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, including duties to act in good faith and in its best interests, to avoid placing himself in positions of conflict, and to disclose information of concern to the company.

The plaintiffs alleged that on 18 February 2002, while still employed within the ABB Singapore Group, Sher Hock Guan Charles' brother and two others incorporated Great Vision International Pte Ltd, and that two months after leaving ABB's employ, Sher Hock Guan Charles became a director and shareholder of Great Vision.

The plaintiffs alleged that on 24 May 2002, while still employed within the ABB Singapore Group, a company called Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd was incorporated, and that Sher Hock Guan Charles became a director and shareholder of Webmoney two months after leaving ABB's employ.

The plaintiffs alleged that in December 2002, while still employed within the ABB Singapore Group, Sher Hock Guan Charles exchanged email correspondence with former ABB Group employee Guenther Leonhardt concerning XIHARI's enquiries about engaging a technical adviser for research and development projects relating to a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.

Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, nine emails were exchanged between Sher Hock Guan Charles and Guenther Leonhardt regarding XIHARI's interest in engaging Leonhardt as a technical adviser and technical controller for a manufacturing company to develop and market medium voltage products.

The plaintiffs alleged that Sher Hock Guan Charles did not disclose to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd or ABB Industry Pte Ltd his communications with XIHARI and Guenther Leonhardt, his involvement in the establishment of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd, or the potential competitive risk posed by XIHARI and Huadian.

The financial statements of Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co Ltd for 2002 recorded an outstanding amount of RMB70,000 owing from 'Charles Sher' described as a travel expenses loan, supported by a payment voucher dated 3 December 2002 showing 'Charles Sher' as payee and applicant and receipt acknowledged by Huang Jianhuang.

The plaintiffs alleged that in March 2003, shortly after leaving ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles was appointed general manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd, a company incorporated on 4 December 2002 with founding shareholders XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd.

The plaintiffs alleged that by 28 March 2004, the shareholders of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd were Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd with 80.5%, XIHARI with 3.3%, and Gelpag mbH with 16.2%, and that Sher Hock Guan Charles was a shareholder of Gelpag mbH.

The plaintiffs alleged that within a few months after its incorporation in December 2002, Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd made rapid progress in manufacturing, marketing, approval and sale of switchgears, circuit breakers and related products similar in characteristics, design, specifications and layout to ABB Group products.
ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd sought relief in the form of damages or an account of profits from Sher Hock Guan Charles based on alleged breaches of contractual and fiduciary duties.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sher Hock Guan Charles, as a director and former president of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, owed contractual and fiduciary duties to that company arising from his employment contracts and the Employee Handbook, and whether his conduct in assisting XIHARI and Huadian and in preparing to join Huadian before resignation gave rise to liability to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe fiduciary duties to their company, including duties of loyalty, good faith, avoidance of conflicts between personal interests and company interests, and duties to disclose information of concern to the company; contractual duties arise from the express and implied terms of employment contracts and incorporated policies, and may include obligations not to participate in other business activities and to protect confidential information; the scope of any fiduciary duty is determined by the nature of the director's role and the company's business.
Party_Details.Application: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd operated in low voltage products and did not itself manufacture medium voltage products, while the alleged competitive threat related to medium voltage products developed and marketed by Huadian; although Sher Hock Guan Charles remained a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd until February 2003, the evidence showed that his substantive management role and the relevant business activities concerning medium voltage products were centred in ABB Industry Pte Ltd; the court assessed whether the non-disclosure of XIHARI's plans and the assistance given to secure a technical adviser for Huadian had a sufficient nexus to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd's business and interests to constitute actionable breaches, taking into account that its operations were confined to low voltage products and that its geographical market differed from the Chinese-focused development activities.
Party_Details.Conclusion: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd obtained judgment against Sher Hock Guan Charles for either damages to be assessed or an account of profits to be taken, at its election, together with ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge recognised that Sher Hock Guan Charles, as a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary duties to that company, but noted that its business was confined to low voltage products and that it did not operate in the medium voltage segment in China; the court analysed the pleaded breaches and the factual matrix, including the development of Huadian's medium voltage products and the defendant's communications with XIHARI and Leonhardt, and concluded that the core actionable breaches related to non-disclosure of XIHARI's plans and assistance to a potential competitor, which affected both the second and third plaintiffs; although the second plaintiff's direct competitive exposure was more limited, the judge treated the fiduciary obligations in relation to the information and assistance as owed to both operating companies and therefore granted them the same form of relief, leaving the precise quantum and allocation to be determined at a later assessment.

Metadata.Sector: Corporate / Employment / Fiduciary Duties / Technology Manufacturing

Party_Details.Name: ABB Industry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: ABB Industry Pte Ltd is part of the ABB Singapore Group and carries on business as, among other things, a manufacturer of switchgears and an installer of electrical works.
ABB Industry Pte Ltd is a wholly owned subsidiary of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd.

From January 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd as General Manager, and from September 2001 to February 2003 he was also Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South.
ABB Industry Pte Ltd's business was mainly concerned with low voltage products, but it also supplied medium voltage products as part of project packages when requested.

ABB Industry Pte Ltd participated in a Country Management Team whose meetings discussed various businesses including medium voltage products, and Sher Hock Guan Charles attended these meetings as part of top management.

On 15 January 2001 ABB Industry Pte Ltd issued a letter to Sher Hock Guan Charles transferring his employment from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd with retrospective effect from 1 January 2001, designating him as General Manager BAU LV Products and Systems with job grade M1.

The 15 January 2001 letter from ABB Industry Pte Ltd was accompanied by a 'Summary of Terms and Conditions of Employment – Staff Category M1/M2' which included a clause reserving management's right to review, change, amend, delete or add to policies, terms and conditions of employment.

The Employee Handbook applicable to ABB Industry Pte Ltd contained clauses prohibiting private participation in other business activities related to the nature of company business, requiring employees to protect company assets and confidential information, and requiring employees to avoid financial, business or other relationships that might be opposed to the company's interests or cause conflicts with their duties.

As a very senior employee and part of the top management of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, with authority to hire and fire employees and responsibility for general management and business development, Sher Hock Guan Charles occupied a position that gave rise to fiduciary duties towards ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

Sher Hock Guan Charles' responsibilities at ABB Industry Pte Ltd included general management of the business and business development, marketing and sales of installation materials and low voltage products in the Asia South region.

On 3 March 2003, shortly after his employment with ABB Industry Pte Ltd ended in February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles commenced employment as General Manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China.

In late October 2002, while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles attended the 10th anniversary celebrations of ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd in China, where he met XIHARI representatives including Li Zhao Lin and Pei Zhengjiang and learned that XIHARI was looking for a technical adviser for research and development projects.

In November 2002, XIHARI's General Manager Pei Zhengjiang and another officer Zheng Jun had telephone conversations with Sher Hock Guan Charles in which they expressed interest in engaging an internationally known technical adviser and mentioned Guenther Leonhardt as a candidate.

Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles exchanged nine emails with former ABB Group engineer and director Guenther Leonhardt, communicating XIHARI's interest in engaging Leonhardt as a technical adviser and technical controller for a manufacturing company to develop a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.

During the email correspondence with Guenther Leonhardt, Sher Hock Guan Charles was aware that XIHARI intended to develop a new generation of medium voltage products to be marketed first in China and potentially in other Asian markets, and that XIHARI sought Leonhardt's expertise to ensure the products met international standards.

Sher Hock Guan Charles did not inform ABB Industry Pte Ltd of XIHARI's plans to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and switchgear or of his role in facilitating contact between XIHARI and Guenther Leonhardt.

In November 2002, XIHARI's General Manager Pei Zhengjiang travelled to Singapore and met Sher Hock Guan Charles to ask him to check with Guenther Leonhardt on the possibility of engaging Leonhardt as XIHARI's technical adviser.

After accepting an offer to join Huadian, Sher Hock Guan Charles spent time before his resignation from ABB Industry Pte Ltd considering and preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian's operations.

At Huadian's first board meeting on 3 March 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles made a slide presentation containing forecasts and proposals for Huadian's business, which the board referred to in its resolutions.

The timing and content of the business plan presented by Sher Hock Guan Charles to Huadian's board on 3 March 2003 indicated that preparatory work on the plan had been undertaken before his arrival in Xiamen on 2 March 2003.

Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd was established as a joint venture between Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd and XIHARI, following a joint design project between Yong Ying (Xiamen) Science and Technology Co Ltd and XIHARI to develop medium voltage vacuum circuit breakers with oval moulded poles.

By 31 January 2003, Huadian had finished prototyping and the most critical type testing of its vacuum circuit breaker, with type testing having begun on 25 October 2002 and completed on 13 March 2003.

On 5 or 6 March 2003, after joining Huadian, Sher Hock Guan Charles invited Guenther Leonhardt to visit Huadian's premises in Xiamen, and Leonhardt subsequently agreed to become Huadian's technical consultant under a consultancy agreement backdated to 1 March 2003.

In October 2003, a German company Gelpag mbH was incorporated with 85% of its shares owned by Sher Hock Guan Charles and 10% by Guenther Leonhardt, and in late March 2004 Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of the shares in Huadian at par value.

Huadian had a registered capital of RMB30 million, and Gelpag mbH's 16.2% shareholding corresponded to an investment of approximately RMB4.86 million, of which Sher Hock Guan Charles' indirect interest was about RMB4.374 million.

Sher Hock Guan Charles stated in evidence that the approximately SGD900,000 he invested via Gelpag mbH in Huadian came from profits he had made from the Xiamen property development project undertaken by Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng.

The ABB Group later observed that Huadian was advertising and offering for sale medium voltage products that were similar in design, specifications, layout and function to ABB's medium voltage products and that Huadian was marketing these products in Southeast Asia and other regions.
During cross-examination, Sher Hock Guan Charles agreed that Huadian was a competitor of ABB companies in the medium voltage products market.

ABB Industry Pte Ltd sought relief in the form of damages or an account of profits from Sher Hock Guan Charles based on alleged breaches of contractual and fiduciary duties arising from his conduct in relation to XIHARI and Huadian.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Sher Hock Guan Charles, as a very senior manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary and contractual duties to disclose XIHARI's plans to develop and market medium voltage products and to refrain from assisting XIHARI and Huadian while still employed, and whether his non-disclosure and preparatory work for Huadian before resignation created liability to ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior managers who occupy positions analogous to directors may owe fiduciary duties similar to those of directors, including duties to act in good faith in the best interests of the company, to avoid conflicts between personal interests and company interests, and to disclose to the company information that comes to them and is of concern to the company; an employee's duty of fidelity includes not secretly assisting a competing business while still employed; contractual terms and incorporated policies can prohibit private participation in other business activities and require protection of company interests and information.
Party_Details.Application: The court considered that ABB Industry Pte Ltd, although primarily a low voltage products company, did sell medium voltage products as part of project packages and that its management, including Sher Hock Guan Charles, discussed medium voltage business at Country Management Team meetings; it found that his senior role and authority placed him in a fiduciary position vis-à-vis ABB Industry Pte Ltd; the evidence showed that while still employed he knew of XIHARI's intention to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and that he actively facilitated contact between XIHARI and Guenther Leonhardt, a highly experienced medium voltage engineer, with a view to securing Leonhardt's services as technical adviser and technical controller for XIHARI and the manufacturing company; he did not inform ABB Industry Pte Ltd of these developments and instead assisted a potential competitor; the timing and substance of his business plan presentation to Huadian's board on 3 March 2003 indicated that he had spent time before his resignation preparing plans and forecasts for Huadian's business, which constituted work for a prospective competitor while still employed; these actions were assessed against his fiduciary duties and his contractual obligations under the Employee Handbook and employment terms not to engage in private participation in other business activities and to avoid conflicts of interest.
Party_Details.Conclusion: ABB Industry Pte Ltd obtained judgment against Sher Hock Guan Charles for either damages to be assessed or an account of profits to be taken, at its election, together with ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge first determined that the Employee Handbook formed part of the employment terms and that Sher Hock Guan Charles, as General Manager and Vice-President heading a business area, was part of ABB Industry Pte Ltd's top management and owed it fiduciary duties akin to those of a director; the court rejected the argument that his fiduciary obligations were confined strictly to low voltage products, noting his involvement in broader management discussions and the company's occasional supply of medium voltage products; it then analysed the email correspondence and meetings with XIHARI and Leonhardt, concluding that he knew XIHARI intended to develop and market medium voltage products that could compete in ABB Industry Pte Ltd's markets and that he nonetheless assisted XIHARI in securing a key technical adviser without informing his employer; drawing on authorities such as British Midland and Cooley, the judge reasoned that his duty to act in the company's best interests included a duty to inform it of activities that could damage those interests and not to assist such activities while still employed; the court also inferred from the sophistication and timing of the Huadian business plan presented on 3 March 2003 that he had undertaken preparatory work for Huadian before leaving ABB Industry Pte Ltd, which was inconsistent with his duty of fidelity; on this basis, the judge identified specific breaches relating to non-disclosure of XIHARI's plans, assistance in engaging Leonhardt, and pre-resignation work on Huadian's business, and ordered that ABB Industry Pte Ltd be granted a remedy in the form of damages or an account of profits.

Metadata.Sector: Corporate / Employment / Fiduciary Duties / Technology Manufacturing

Party_Details.Name: Sher Hock Guan Charles

Facts.Text: Sher Hock Guan Charles is a Singaporean who worked for various companies in the ABB Group between 1990 and February 2003.
During his ABB career, Sher Hock Guan Charles worked in Xiamen, China for ABB China Ltd and ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd before being based in Singapore from January 1997.
From January 1997 to January 2000, Sher Hock Guan Charles served as President of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
From July 1998 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd.
From January 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles was General Manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

From September 2001 to February 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles held the position of Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, in ABB Industry Pte Ltd.
Sher Hock Guan Charles' employment with the ABB Singapore Group was governed by three successive employment contracts dated 19 July 1996, 20 September 1999, and 15 January 2001.

The 19 July 1996 employment contract between ABB Holdings Pte Ltd and Sher Hock Guan Charles covered the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000 and required him to use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.

The 20 September 1999 employment contract, effective 1 February 2000, provided that Sher Hock Guan Charles would maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters, not divulge information to outside members, and that private participation in other business activities, especially in ABB's line of business, was strictly not allowed.

The 15 January 2001 letter from ABB Industry Pte Ltd transferred Sher Hock Guan Charles' employment from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd with effect from 1 January 2001 and attached a Summary of Terms and Conditions of Employment for staff category M1/M2.

The Employee Handbook used by the ABB Singapore Group contained provisions stating that private participation in other business activities especially related to the nature of company business was strictly not allowed, that employees must protect company assets and confidential and proprietary information, and that employees must avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or cause conflicts with their duties.
In evidence, Sher Hock Guan Charles acknowledged that he was aware of and had access to the Employee Handbook at all material times and that it governed his practice in the ABB companies.

As a senior manager and director within the ABB Singapore Group, Sher Hock Guan Charles attended Country Management Team meetings where various business segments, including medium voltage products, were discussed.
On 15 January 2001, Sher Hock Guan Charles' employment was transferred from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd with retrospective effect from 1 January 2001.
Sher Hock Guan Charles' employment with ABB Industry Pte Ltd ended in February 2003.
On 3 March 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles commenced employment as General Manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China.
In March 2004, after Gelpag mbH acquired shares in Huadian, Sher Hock Guan Charles became Managing Director of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd.
Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd is a manufacturer of switchgears and circuit breakers, particularly medium voltage circuit breakers.

On 18 February 2002, Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore with initial shareholders and directors including Sher Hock Guan Charles' brother, Sher Hock Sing, and two others.

Great Vision International Pte Ltd was set up to undertake a property development project in Xiamen, China through a subsidiary project company, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co Ltd.

In February 2002, Sher Hock Guan Charles learned from Xiamen Mayor Hong Yong Shi about an opportunity to develop a plot of land in Xiamen and passed this opportunity to his brother and business associate Lin Qunxing.

On 25 April 2003, approximately two months after leaving ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles was appointed a director of Great Vision International Pte Ltd and acquired 153 shares in the company from his brother, who resigned as director.
Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co Ltd was established in July 2002 with Great Vision International Pte Ltd holding 90% of its shares and a Chinese company holding the remaining 10%.

The 2002 financial statements of Xiamen Fu Cheng recorded an outstanding amount of RMB70,000 owing from 'Charles Sher' described as a travel expenses loan, supported by a payment voucher dated 3 December 2002 listing 'Charles Sher' as payee and applicant and showing receipt acknowledged by Huang Jianhuang.

Sher Hock Guan Charles stated that the RMB70,000 recorded in Xiamen Fu Cheng's accounts was a cash advance for travel expenses incurred by his brother and Huang Jianhuang and that his name was entered by mistake.
On 24 May 2002, Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore with directors Luo Baocheng and Chen Wei Ting, and its registered address was Sher Hock Guan Charles' home address.
On 25 April 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles became a director and shareholder of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd, holding one SGD1 paid-up share.

Sher Hock Guan Charles stated that he assisted in incorporating Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd to help his friend Luo Baocheng with a proposed reverse takeover and that he held his share as Luo's nominee without investing his own funds.
Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd remained dormant and did not carry out any business after its incorporation.
In late October 2002, while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd, Sher Hock Guan Charles attended the 10th anniversary celebrations of ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China.

During the October 2002 event in Xiamen, Sher Hock Guan Charles spoke with XIHARI representatives, including Chairman Li Zhao Lin and General Manager Pei Zhengjiang, and learned that XIHARI was seeking a technical adviser for research and development projects.

In November 2002, XIHARI's General Manager Pei Zhengjiang and officer Zheng Jun had several telephone conversations with Sher Hock Guan Charles in which they expressed interest in engaging an internationally known technical adviser and mentioned former ABB engineer and director Guenther Leonhardt as a candidate.

Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, nine emails were exchanged between Sher Hock Guan Charles and Guenther Leonhardt concerning XIHARI's proposal that Leonhardt act as technical adviser for research and development projects on a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters and as technical controller for a manufacturing company.

Sher Hock Guan Charles believed at the time that XIHARI, as a Chinese research institute, would not pose a potential threat or competitive risk to the business of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd or ABB Industry Pte Ltd.

Sher Hock Guan Charles did not inform ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd or ABB Industry Pte Ltd about XIHARI's intention to develop a new generation of medium voltage products or about his role in facilitating contact between XIHARI and Guenther Leonhardt.
In January 2003, Chinese businessman Liu Chunguang approached Sher Hock Guan Charles to offer him the position of General Manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co Ltd.
By January 2003, Sher Hock Guan Charles had decided to leave ABB Industry Pte Ltd and had informed senior ABB officer Tom Sjoekvist of his intention to resign.

In discussing terms with Liu Chunguang, Sher Hock Guan Charles was offered up to 20% of the shares in Huadian, which he could purchase at par value, and he agreed to join Huadian as General Manager with effect from 3 March 2003.

On 3 March 2003, at Huadian's first board meeting, Sher Hock Guan Charles made a presentation containing forecasts and proposals for Huadian's business, which the board referred to in its resolutions.

The content and detail of the business plan presented by Sher Hock Guan Charles to Huadian's board on 3 March 2003 suggested that preparatory work on the plan had been undertaken before his arrival in Xiamen on 2 March 2003.

On 5 or 6 March 2003, after joining Huadian, Sher Hock Guan Charles telephoned Guenther Leonhardt and invited him to visit Huadian's premises in Xiamen to consider becoming Huadian's technical consultant.

Following his visit to Xiamen, Guenther Leonhardt agreed to become Huadian's technical consultant, and a consultancy agreement between Leonhardt and Huadian was signed in late March 2003 and backdated to 1 March 2003.

In October 2003, Gelpag mbH was incorporated in Germany with 85% of its shares owned by Sher Hock Guan Charles and 10% by Guenther Leonhardt, and in late March 2004 Gelpag mbH acquired a 16.2% shareholding in Huadian.

Sher Hock Guan Charles testified that the funds he used to invest in Gelpag mbH and indirectly in Huadian were derived from profits he had earned from the Xiamen property development project undertaken by Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng.
During cross-examination, Sher Hock Guan Charles agreed that Huadian was a competitor of ABB companies in the medium voltage products market.

In December 2007, ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd commenced Suit 798 of 2007 against Sher Hock Guan Charles alleging breaches of contractual, implied and fiduciary duties arising from his conduct in relation to other businesses and Huadian.

In his defence, Sher Hock Guan Charles denied owing the implied and fiduciary duties pleaded by the plaintiffs, save for specific clauses in the 1996 and 1999 employment agreements, and denied that his conduct in relation to Great Vision, Webmoney, XIHARI and Huadian breached any duties owed to the plaintiffs.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, on the facts established prior to judicial determination, Sher Hock Guan Charles' roles, contracts and conduct created contractual and fiduciary obligations towards the ABB plaintiffs, and whether his non-disclosure of XIHARI's plans, assistance in securing a technical adviser for XIHARI and Huadian, and preparatory work for Huadian while still employed exposed him to liability for damages or an account of profits.
Party_Details.Rule: Employment contracts and incorporated policies define express and implied duties of employees, including duties of secrecy, non-participation in competing business activities, and protection of company interests; directors and very senior managers may owe fiduciary duties to act in good faith in the best interests of their company, to avoid conflicts between personal interests and company interests, and to disclose to the company information that is of concern to it; an employee's duty of fidelity prohibits secretly assisting a competing business while still employed; remedies for breach of such duties can include damages or an account of profits.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the three employment contracts and the Employee Handbook to determine the scope of Sher Hock Guan Charles' contractual obligations, concluding that the Handbook formed part of his terms and that he was prohibited from private participation in other business activities related to ABB's business and required to protect confidential information and avoid conflicts of interest; it then assessed his positions as director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and senior manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, finding that these roles placed him in a fiduciary position vis-à-vis the operating companies; the factual record showed that while still employed he knew of XIHARI's intention to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage products, facilitated contact between XIHARI and former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt to secure Leonhardt's services as technical adviser and technical controller for XIHARI and the manufacturing company, and did not inform his ABB employers of these developments; the evidence also indicated that he undertook preparatory work on Huadian's business plan before his resignation, as reflected in the detailed presentation to Huadian's board on 3 March 2003; these actions were evaluated against his contractual and fiduciary duties to determine whether they constituted assistance to a potential competitor and non-disclosure of information of concern to his employers while he was still in their service.
Party_Details.Conclusion: Judgment was entered against Sher Hock Guan Charles in favour of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd for either damages to be assessed or an account of profits to be taken at their election, while the claim of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd was dismissed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge first resolved the contractual framework, holding that the Employee Handbook was incorporated into the employment contracts and that the onerous 'best endeavours to promote the ABB Group' clause in the first contract did not carry over into later contracts; she then determined that Sher Hock Guan Charles, as a director of the second plaintiff and a top-level manager of the third plaintiff, owed fiduciary duties to those operating companies but not to the holding company; the court analysed the various business ventures, finding insufficient evidence that he actively participated in the Xiamen property project or Webmoney in a way that breached his duties, and noting that those ventures were outside ABB's line of business; turning to Huadian, the judge accepted that the technical development of Huadian's products was driven by Yong Ying and XIHARI and that the defendant lacked the technical knowledge to contribute to product design or to misappropriate ABB's technical information; however, she concluded that his knowledge of XIHARI's plans, his role in facilitating the engagement of Leonhardt for XIHARI and Huadian, and his pre-resignation work on Huadian's business plan constituted breaches of his fiduciary duty to disclose information of concern to his employers and his duty of fidelity not to assist a potential competitor while still employed; the court rejected arguments that XIHARI's status as a research institute or the alleged 'low-end' nature of its products removed the potential competitive risk, pointing to Huadian's stated ambition to reach international standards and compete globally; on this basis, the judge limited liability to specific breaches related to XIHARI and Huadian and ordered that the second and third plaintiffs be granted a choice between damages and an account of profits, with quantum and costs to be determined at a later stage.
